claims.text: Andrew Bashiago claims that, beginning in 1968 at age six, he was sent through a working teleportation/time-travel device built from Nicola Tesla's stolen papers and successfully traveled back to Lincoln's Gettysburg Address as a chronaut for a secret DARPA program called Project Pegasus.

evidence.quote:
1968, a six-year-old boy stepped
through a shimmering curtain of light
and vanished.
When Andrew Bashiago's vision cleared,
he was no longer in an empty airplane
hanger in New Jersey. He was standing in
a muddy field.

Andrew Bashago traveled back to 1863 and
returned with memories that were sharp,
vivid, and historically accurate. For
the scientists at Project Pegasus, this
was the breakthrough that they needed.

claims.rationale: Bashiago's chronaut narrative is a real public claim he has made for over a decade in books, podcasts, and on Coast to Coast AM, but the underlying physics (no working time-travel device exists), the absence of any documentary record of 'Project Pegasus,' and Bashiago's own shifting timeline make the literal claim almost certainly false. The host himself debunks it later in the video. Confidence is high that he asserts it; directTruth is very low.

claims.text: On January 7, 1943 Nicola Tesla died alone at the New Yorker Hotel and US government agents seized roughly 80 boxes of his papers within hours, with about 20 boxes never returned to his family — a portion of which the FBI still keeps classified to this day.

evidence.quote:
Nicola Tesla died
alone in room 3327 of the New Yorker
Hotel. Within hours, government agents
sealed the room. They confiscated
everything. 80 boxes of ideas,
inventions, and decades of research.
When Tesla's belongings were returned to
his family, 20 boxes were missing. The
FBI still has classified Tesla documents

claims.rationale: Tesla's death on Jan 7, 1943 in room 3327 of the New Yorker Hotel is well-documented. The Office of Alien Property (not the FBI directly, though the FBI was involved) seized his papers; the volume figures and number of missing boxes vary in different sources but the basic pattern of seizure-and-incomplete-return is corroborated. The host endorses this in his own debunk section as 'documented.'

claims.text: The 1957 Soviet launch of Sputnik shocked the United States into believing it was technologically behind, prompting President Eisenhower to create ARPA (later DARPA) with a standing mandate to ensure America was never again caught off-guard by foreign technology.

evidence.quote:
pace? The course of
United States policies in the
competition with Russia has been
severely shaken. Why and how did the
Russians beat us to the draw? We had
better get on our tour. We must see to
it that whatever advantages they have
are temporary only. The Soviets had
launched Sputnik, the f

ARPA, the Advanced Research
Projects Agency. Later, they'd add
defense and become DARPA. Their mandate
was simple. Make sure America was never
surprised by technology again.
DARPA had unlimited funding

claims.rationale: Sputnik launched October 4, 1957 and ARPA was established in February 1958 in direct response, with the explicit mission of preventing technological surprise. This is canonical Cold War / DOD history. 'Unlimited funding' is hyperbole — ARPA's budget was real but bounded — but the founding rationale is accurately stated.

claims.text: DARPA secretly built a working teleportation device in the basement of the Curtis Wright building in Wood-Ridge, New Jersey from Tesla's confiscated designs — a two-pillar machine that drew on zero-point energy and could move people through space and time.

evidence.quote:
most secret program in
America. They were building Tesla's
teleportation device.
Teleportation was more powerful than any
nuclear weapon. With this technology,
America could put a million

Tesla had figured out how to tap
into it. Dr. Harold Agnu assembled the
machine exactly to Tesla's
specifications. Every measurement, every
angle, everything matched the stolen
designs. And looking at it, he was sure
something was missing. This couldn't
possibly wo

claims.rationale: There is no credible evidence that any such teleportation lab existed at Curtis Wright or anywhere else; Tesla's wireless-power papers do not contain teleportation designs; zero-point energy cannot be tapped at the scales needed (the host says so in his own debunk). The narrative is part of Bashiago/conspiracy lore, not documented history. The host frames it as the Bashiago story, then dismantles the science.

dependencies.rationale: If the device never existed, Bashiago's chronaut trips couldn't have occurred.

claims.text: Adult test subjects sent through the device were killed or driven insane — Lt. Morrison vanished, Lt. Farley aged 30 years in 30 seconds, Lt. Hutchkins materialized in disjointed body parts — leading scientists to conclude that only children's pliable minds could survive teleportation, justifying the recruitment of six-year-olds into the program.

evidence.quote:
Lieutenant Morrison
went first. 26 years old, road scholar,
perfect physical condition. He stepped
through the shimmer and vanished. The
receiving team in New Mexico waited 5
minutes, 10 minutes, an hour. Morrison
never arrived. Not in New Mexico, not
anywhere. The best physicist in the

Hutchkins arrived in
pieces. Not torn apart, separated in
time. His legs materialized first, his
torso 30 seconds later, arms 2 minutes
after that. The medical team stood by
helplessly. His heart had arrived before
his lungs. His brain was sending signals
to limbs tha

very
instinct told him this was wrong. But
the data was clear and they were out of
options and out of adult volunteers. The
children were their only chance.
[Music]
[Music]
The lab at Curtis Wright looked like any
government facility. Gray

claims.rationale: These vivid casualty anecdotes are part of the Bashiago/Pegasus folklore (often retold by Andrew Bashiago and Stewart Swerdlow) but no military records, casualty reports, or contemporaneous documentation back any of them. Names like Morrison/Farley/Hutchkins do not appear in any verifiable DOD roster for such tests. As narrative the host plainly relays it; as fact it is essentially fabricated.

claims.text: By 1980 Project Pegasus had reportedly graduated from time travel to space travel via 'jump rooms' housed in an unmarked El Segundo office building, sending teen chronauts — including (per Bashiago) a young Barack Obama under the alias 'Barry Soetoro' — to a Mars populated by plesiosaur-like and tall-grey-alien civilizations.

evidence.quote:
Mars isn't the
barren, lifeless world NASA shows us. In
certain places, it had breathable air,
and in those places, there was life on
Mars.
Wonder if you'll never know. He's in the
bes

tall, thin beings
with large heads and black eyes. The
mission was to beat the Soviets to Mars
and establish contact with this advanced
civilization. And Andrew wasn't going
alone. The Mars program recruited young
people who'd proven themselves in the
time travel ex

Barry Storo. Barry was a
lanky college student from Columbia
University. Friendly guy, articulate,
easy smile. Years later, the world would
know him by a different name. Barack
Obama. Wait, wait, wait. What? What?
Barack Obama was a Martian cryonaut.
Yep. Wasn't Obama also invited to the
Bilde

claims.rationale: The 'Obama went to Mars' claim was actually pushed by Bashiago and Andrew D. Basiago around 2012 and was officially denied by the White House (Press Secretary Tommy Vietor: 'only if you count Mars Bars'). Mars rovers have found no civilizations or breathable air. The 'jump room' building at 999 N. Sepulveda is a real address but contains no such facility. The literal claim is essentially zero-credibility.

claims.text: On a live Coast-to-Coast-style broadcast, host Art Bell appeared to spontaneously confirm a key obscure detail from Bashiago's story when, after Bashiago mentioned ITT Defense Communications in Nutley, New Jersey, Bell admitted he had personally worked there as a technician on a secret government project — a moment fans cite as the most stunning corroboration Bashiago has ever received.

evidence.quote:
m ITT
Defense Communications in Nutley, New
Jersey. The broadcast went silent. Not
dead air, stun silence. When Art spoke
again, his voice had changed. The
confident host was gone. I encountered
you w

spontaneous unprompted confirmation of
a key obscure detail in Bashiago's
story. Art was floored. You can hear him
trying to reconcile the impossibility of
what he was hearing.
I indeed work for ITT in Nutley, New
Jersey. So,
Oh my god. I I just I Okay, that's a
bomb. That's a bombshell. Okay. Okay.

claims.rationale: The on-air exchange between Bell and Bashiago is real and audible in archived broadcasts. However the host's later debunk section notes that Art Bell had publicly disclosed his ITT Nutley employment in his 1998 autobiography 'The Art of Talk,' so Bashiago could simply have read it. The exchange happened; whether it constitutes independent confirmation is what's contested.

claims.text: Believers point to Josephine Cobb's 1952-discovered photograph — long the only confirmed image of Lincoln at Gettysburg — and the small, oddly-modern-haircut boy in oversized adult shoes visible in the foreground, identifying him as the time-traveling six-year-old Bashiago.

evidence.quote:
photograph of Abraham Lincoln at
Gettysburg. They knew photographers were
there. They knew the equipment existed,
but no confirmed image of Lincoln giving
his most famous speech had ever been
found.
In 1952, archivist Josephine Cobb was
examining

and soldiers,
could be seen in the background, but in
the foreground, wearing an itchy blue
uniform and oversized shoes, is
six-year-old Andrew Bashiago.
The story of Andrew Bashiago is
compelling. He's intelligent,
articulate, he knows how to tell a
story. But is it true? No. No

claims.rationale: The Cobb photograph (NARA, 1863 Mathew Brady plate) is real and was the only widely-known Lincoln-at-Gettysburg image for many decades. The 'mystery boy' figure does appear in the photograph, but Bashiago-as-the-boy is an unfalsifiable identification with no chain of custody. The supposedly anachronistic haircut and shoes are also visible on many other 1860s children in period photos.

dependencies.rationale: The photograph is the keystone alleged physical evidence for Bashiago's chronaut claim.

claims.text: The host's own scientific debunk: Tesla's radiant-energy work was about wireless power transmission, not teleportation; even quantum-state teleportation of a person would require more energy than an exploding star; and any literal time-jump ignores that the moving Earth, solar system, and galaxy mean a 5-second jump would leave you in empty space.

evidence.quote:
wireless power transmission, not
teleportation. Yeah. But but but but he
teleported Wolverine a bunch of times uh
during that magic show. You're talking
about the movie The Prestige, right? And
who played Tesla in that movie? David
Bowie, right? And who played

scientists have teleported quantum
states of particles, but teleporting a
person, that would require more energy
than an exploding star, even if they
could figure out how to do it. Bashiago
says they just plug the machine into the
w

Earth, the solar system, the galaxy,
they're all constantly moving. If you
time travel 5 seconds, you'll be in the
middle of space. Nobody ever accounts
for this. Nobody except nobody except
Mike Madman Markham. He factored this
into his calculations. That's why his
time travel story is prob

claims.rationale: All three points are mainstream physics. Tesla's Colorado Springs experiments were RF wireless power, not matter teleportation. Quantum teleportation has only been done with single particles' quantum states, never with macroscopic objects, and the energy/information bandwidth scales catastrophically. The reference frame problem is also a real, frequently-overlooked flaw in pop time-travel stories. The host's debunk here is solid.

dependencies.rationale: Real Tesla physics doesn't permit a wall-socket teleporter.

claims.text: Bashiago's specific factual claims fail under scrutiny: he names Donald Rumsfeld as Pegasus's director from 1969–1972, but Rumsfeld is documented to have been running domestic policy then with no DARPA connection; Art Bell's ITT Nutley employment was already public in his 1998 autobiography; and Bashiago's recruitment date, trip count, and Mars-jump dates have shifted across his retellings.

evidence.quote:
Donald Rumsfeld
ran the project from 1969 to 1972, but
Rumsfeld was working on domestic policy
then, nowhere near DARPA, and that's
documented. The Art Bell confirmation
seems stunning until you realize Art
mentioned working at ITT Nut

gh Bowie for one show. Never
enough Bowie.
Sure. Anyway, he published a paper
claiming to discover life on Mars by
analyzing NASA photos. He said he saw
humanoids, animals, and structures, but
he never mentioned he'd been there
himself. Why analyze blurry photos if
you've walked on Mars? He only added the
jump room story years later. Oh, so he
probably didn't blow up

claims.rationale: Rumsfeld was Director of the Office of Economic Opportunity 1969–1970 and Counsellor to the President 1969–1973 — domestic policy, exactly as the host says. Art Bell's autobiography 'The Art of Talk' (1998) does mention his ITT employment, undermining the 'unprompted confirmation' framing. Bashiago's 2008 paper 'The Discovery of Life on Mars' analyzed NASA photos with no mention of a personal visit; the jump-room narrative was added later. All three internal inconsistencies are well-documented in skeptic literature.

dependencies.rationale: Public prior disclosure undermines the supposedly spontaneous on-air confirmation.

claims.text: Bashiago fits a recognizable pattern of self-proclaimed 'secret space program' veterans — they all claim multiple programs, alien encounters, and suppressed memories, and they all monetize the story through paid talks, seminars, and books — which the host treats as a structural reason for skepticism even before the physics objections.

evidence.quote:
ails, there's always the same
answer. Their memories were suppressed,
but now they're slowly coming back. And
these storytellers share something else.
They turn their stories into income. Why
couldn't someone else talk about their
experience in a secret space program? As
long as they don't talk about the stuff
that I created ba

claims.rationale: The 'secret space program' subculture (Corey Goode, Randy Cramer, Michael Relfe, Bashiago, Stewart Swerdlow, etc.) really does share these structural features — multi-program claims, recovered/suppressed memories, paid speaking circuits, and self-published books. The host's 'follow the money' heuristic is a valid epistemic flag, though not by itself decisive proof of fabrication.

claims.text: The host closes with an explicit moral thesis: time-travel stories appeal to the universal human craving for a 'second chance' to undo regrets, but no such loophole exists outside the present — accept the past, learn from it, and the only real time machine is today.

evidence.quote:
Your second chance isn't in some secret
government program. It's not hidden in a
classified file or a stolen Tesla
blueprint. Your second chance starts the
second you stop looking backward and
start looking forward. The time machine
you're looking for, yo

claims.rationale: This is the host's closing personal/philosophical thesis, not an empirical claim, so it can't be 'true' or 'false' in the usual sense. It is well-asserted (the verdict-section sign-off), so confidence is high but I omit directTruth.
